text.title: Transaction Patterns and Fault Tolerance
text.summary: 2PC vs Saga vs TCC trade-offs, Circuit Breaker state machine, Bulkhead semaphore vs thread-pool, and Retry with exponential backoff jitter strategies.

rule.statement: Prefer Saga over 2PC for long-running distributed transactions; use 2PC only for short, critical financial operations where strong isolation is legally required.
rule.rationale: 2PC holds database locks for the duration of the transaction across all participants. In a distributed system with network latency, lock duration can be seconds, causing contention. Saga releases locks after each local transaction and uses compensations for rollback.

list.item: 2PC: strong consistency, full isolation, lower availability (coordinator is SPOF), higher latency. Use for: core financial ledger, strict audit, short transactions.
Saga: eventual consistency, no isolation (dirty reads between steps), high availability, lower latency. Use for: order processing, long-running workflows, microservices.
TCC (Try-Confirm-Cancel): partial isolation via reservation phase, medium complexity. Use for: inventory holds, seat reservations, time-sensitive resource allocation.

section.title: Circuit Breaker

rule.statement: Configure circuit breaker failure threshold as an absolute count within a rolling time window, not as a percentage, for low-traffic services.
rule.rationale: At low traffic (5 req/min), a 50% failure rate threshold opens the circuit after 2-3 failures — correct for high traffic but too sensitive for low traffic where single errors are noise. Absolute count (e.g., 5 failures in 60s) is more appropriate.

section.p: Typical config: failure threshold 5-10 in a 10-60s window; open timeout 30-60s; success threshold in half-open 3-5; slow call threshold treated as failure when duration exceeds 2x expected p99.

rule.statement: Use thread-pool bulkhead when timeout isolation is required; use semaphore bulkhead when overhead must be minimal and the calling thread can be blocked safely.
rule.rationale: Thread-pool isolation allows explicit timeouts for waiting requests — the pool rejects new requests when full. Semaphore isolation runs on the calling thread, so a blocked semaphore blocks the caller's thread with no timeout option.

section.title: Retry with Jitter

rule.statement: Apply decorrelated jitter (next_wait = min(cap, random(base, prev_wait * 3))) rather than pure exponential backoff when many clients retry the same service simultaneously.
rule.rationale: Pure exponential backoff synchronises all clients to retry at exactly the same intervals, creating thundering herd. Decorrelated jitter breaks the synchronisation by randomising each client's wait independently.

section.p: Jitter strategies: Full Jitter = random(0, base * 2^attempt), spreads all retries; Equal Jitter = delay/2 + random(0, delay/2), moderate spread; Decorrelated = min(cap, random(base, prev * 3)), best for high-contention (AWS/Netflix recommended).

antipattern.description: Retrying non-idempotent write operations without verifying idempotency first.
antipattern.reason: Retrying a POST /orders without idempotency key creates duplicate orders. Either make the operation idempotent via idempotency key, or convert to PUT with a client-generated ID.
